title: Dark caramel
subtitle: Dry-method caramel sauce: bitter-edged, deep amber
excerpt: A dry-method dark caramel taken to 175°C before the cream goes in. The bitterness at this stage is what separates a good caramel from a sweet one.
sourceNotes: Auguste Escoffier, Le Guide Culinaire (1903), translated edition: caramel and sugar-work technique as the basis of classical French pâtisserie. Jules Gouffé, Le Livre de Cuisine (1867): dry sugar-caramel method and stage temperatures. Urbain Dubois, Cuisine Classique (1856): caramel sauce preparation and use in classical desserts. Melting granulated sugar without added water is the standard professional dry-caramel technique for consistent colour control and avoiding crystallisation.

content.text: A dark caramel is not a sweet sauce. Taken to 175°C, the sugar has already lost much of its sweetness to complex, bitter aromatic compounds. The cream and butter added at the end bring it back toward a sauce, but the bitterness stays. This is what makes the difference between caramel that tastes like burnt toffee and caramel that tastes like nothing else.

This recipe uses the dry method: sugar is melted without any added water. The advantage is control. Without water, there is no risk of crystallisation once the sugar is liquid, and the colour develops faster and more evenly. The disadvantage is speed, dry caramel moves quickly at the critical stage and there is no room to walk away.
Read the method fully before starting. Caramel at 175°C is one of the most hazardous things a home cook works with. It sticks to skin, retains heat, and causes serious burns on contact.
What you need

items.name: Unsalted butter
items.prepNote: cut into cubes, at room temperature

content.text: Use granulated sugar rather than caster sugar. The larger crystals melt at a more controlled rate, which gives you a few extra seconds of observation time. A heavy-based stainless-steel or uncoated pan is correct; non-stick pans are not suitable for sugar work at this temperature.
Method
Warm the cream

Before you begin the caramel, warm {{double-cream}} the cream in a jug until it is steaming. Cold cream added to hot caramel causes a violent reaction that can spit liquid sugar several feet. Warm cream still reacts, but far less aggressively. Keep the jug within arm's reach.
Start the dry caramel

Add about one-third of the {{granulated-sugar}} granulated sugar to a wide, heavy-based pan over medium heat. Do not stir. Wait until the sugar at the edges begins to melt and turn golden, then tilt and swirl the pan gently to move the liquid over the unmelted sugar. This is
caramelisation
beginning. Add another third of the sugar and repeat. Add the final third when most of the previous batch is liquid.

Once all the sugar is liquid, continue to swirl the pan gently. Watch the colour carefully. At 165°C the caramel is pale gold and sweet. At 170°C it turns amber and begins to smell complex and nutty. At 175°C it is dark amber, darker than honey, with a distinctly bitter note. Remove from the heat at 175°C. If you do not have a sugar thermometer, use colour: you want a colour close to dark amber maple syrup, not milk toffee and not near-black.
Add the cream, caution required
If hot syrup touches skin, run cold water over it for at least ten minutes and seek medical care if needed.

Remove the pan from the heat. Standing back slightly, pour the warm cream into the caramel in a slow, steady stream. The mixture will bubble violently and may spit. Continue pouring steadily and stir with the silicone spatula once the worst of the bubbling subsides. If lumps of hardened caramel form at the base, return the pan to low heat and stir until dissolved.
Add the butter and season

Add {{unsalted-butter}} the butter cubes a few at a time, stirring after each addition until fully melted and incorporated. The caramel should be glossy and pourable. If using, stir in the crushed sea-salt flakes. Taste and adjust: if it is too bitter, the sugar went too dark; if it is too sweet with no edge, the sugar did not go dark enough.
Cool and store

Pour the caramel into a heatproof jar or bowl and leave to cool. It will thicken as it cools. A thin, pourable sauce at 80°C will become a thick, spoonable sauce at room temperature. Refrigerated, it sets to a soft, dense texture.
Troubleshooting

attrs.intro: Three problems specific to dry-method caramel.

items.fix: Do not stir; only tilt and swirl the pan. If the batch seizes entirely, add 2 tablespoons of water and heat gently to redissolve, then continue. The wet method (adding water at the start) avoids this issue entirely, though colour control is slightly harder.
items.cause: The sugar was stirred during early melting, which encourages crystals to form and propagate through the liquid.
items.symptom: Sugar crystallises and seizes into a grainy solid mass instead of melting smoothly

items.fix: Return the pan to low heat and stir steadily. The lumps will dissolve given time. Warm the cream before the next attempt.
items.cause: Cream was too cold, causing the caramel to set on contact.
items.symptom: Cream addition causes large solid lumps of caramel that will not stir out

items.fix: Brush down the sides of the pan with a wet pastry brush during cooking to wash any crystals back into the liquid. Use a clean pan without any sugar residue.
items.cause: Sugar re-crystallised during cooking, or the pan had residual sugar crystals on the sides.
items.symptom: Finished caramel is grainy or sandy when cooled

attrs.heading: When things go sideways

content.text: Uses for dark caramel

Dark caramel works anywhere a sweet sauce would be cloying. Serve over vanilla ice cream, spoon into the base of a tart case before filling, stir through buttercream, or layer into a charlotte. The bitterness holds its own against rich, fatty components in a way that pale caramel does not.
